plainEnglish: At Level AAA, don't lock users into one input method. People may switch between touch, mouse, keyboard, and stylus—even mid-task—so your content shouldn't restrict which input modalities work, unless the restriction is essential or needed for security.
whyItMatters: Users who switch input methods—for example from touch to an attached keyboard—are blocked when content is hard-coded to accept only one modality.
quickCheck: Try operating the app with touch, mouse, and keyboard interchangeably and confirm none are artificially disabled.
commonMistakes: Detecting a 'touch device' and then disabling mouse or keyboard handlers.
Building interactions that assume only one input type will ever be used.
Removing hover or keyboard support on touch devices.

codeExamples.label: Supports pointer and keyboard regardless of device
codeExamples.code:
<div
  onPointerDown={handleActivate}
  onKeyDown={(e) => e.key === "Enter" && handleActivate(e)}
  tabIndex={0}
  role="button"
/>

codeExamples.label: Disables keyboard when touch is detected
codeExamples.code:
// Removes keyboard handling on touch devices
if (isTouchDevice) element.onkeydown = null;
